facts.text: Led Zeppelin's 'Stairway to Heaven' has never been released as a single, yet it remains one of the most requested songs in radio history.
Jimi Hendrix was left-handed but played a right-handed Fender Stratocaster flipped upside down and restrung.
Pink Floyd's 'Dark Side of the Moon' spent 937 weeks on the Billboard 200 chart, one of the longest runs ever.
The Rolling Stones got their name from a Muddy Waters song called 'Rollin' Stone.'
Eric Clapton earned the nickname 'Slowhand' because audiences would slow clap while he changed broken guitar strings on stage.
Jimi Hendrix was born on November 27, 1942, in Seattle, Washington.
The Gibson Les Paul Standard was introduced in 1952 and became one of the most iconic electric guitars in rock history.
Woodstock took place on a dairy farm in Bethel, New York, not in the town of Woodstock as commonly believed.
AC/DC's 'Back in Black' is the second best-selling album of all time, with over 50 million copies sold worldwide.
The power chord, a staple of rock music, consists of just the root note and the fifth, with no third to make it major or minor.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Rumours' was recorded while the band members were going through painful breakups with each other.
The Beatles played their last public concert on the rooftop of Apple Corps headquarters in London on January 30, 1969.
Marshall amplifiers were originally created by Jim Marshall to meet the demands of guitarists who wanted louder, more distorted sounds.
Queen's 'Bohemian Rhapsody' was considered too long for radio at nearly six minutes, but became a massive hit anyway.
The British Invasion of the 1960s was led by bands like The Beatles, The Rolling Stones, and The Who, who brought blues-influenced rock to American audiences.
Deep Purple's 'Smoke on the Water' riff is often the first thing beginner guitarists learn to play.
David Bowie created the alter ego Ziggy Stardust in 1972, blending glam rock with science fiction themes.
The Fender Stratocaster, introduced in 1954, featured three pickups and a tremolo bar, revolutionizing electric guitar design.
Black Sabbath is widely considered the first heavy metal band, with their self-titled debut released on Friday the 13th in February 1970.
Elton John's real name is Reginald Kenneth Dwight. He legally changed it in 1972.
The Monterey Pop Festival in 1967 introduced American audiences to Jimi Hendrix, who famously set his guitar on fire during his set.
Abbey Road Studios in London was originally called EMI Recording Studios but was renamed after The Beatles' iconic album.
Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers formed in 1976 in Gainesville, Florida, blending rock with folk and country elements.
The wah pedal, particularly the Cry Baby model, became famous through Jimi Hendrix's use of it on songs like 'Voodoo Child.'
Led Zeppelin never won a Grammy during their active years, despite being one of the biggest bands in history.
Bruce Springsteen's 'Born to Run' album saved his career—Columbia Records was about to drop him before its release.
The Who's Keith Moon was known for destroying his drum kit at the end of nearly every performance.
Creedence Clearwater Revival had five top-ten singles in 1969 alone, more than any other artist that year.
The Doors took their name from Aldous Huxley's book 'The Doors of Perception,' which was itself a reference to William Blake.
Jim Morrison died in Paris on July 3, 1971, at the age of 27, joining what would later be called the 27 Club.
The Gibson SG became Angus Young of AC/DC's signature guitar, known for its lightweight body and aggressive tone.
Storm Thorgerson designed some of rock's most iconic album covers, including Pink Floyd's 'Dark Side of the Moon' prism.
Lynyrd Skynyrd's 'Free Bird' features one of the longest guitar solos in classic rock, running over four minutes.
Neil Young has recorded in genres ranging from folk and country to grunge and electronic music over his 50+ year career.
The Fender Twin Reverb amplifier became a studio staple, prized for its clean tone and built-in spring reverb.
Janis Joplin's raw, emotionally powerful vocals at the Monterey Pop Festival in 1967 made her an instant star.
The Eagles' 'Hotel California' album cover was photographed at the Beverly Hills Hotel at sunset.
Slide guitar, played with a glass or metal tube on the finger, is a technique rooted in Delta blues that became essential to classic rock.
Aerosmith, often called 'the Bad Boys from Boston,' formed in 1970 and became one of America's greatest rock bands.
Pink Floyd's Roger Waters and David Gilmour feuded so bitterly that they rarely appeared together after Waters left in 1985.
The Vox AC30 amplifier was favored by The Beatles and Brian May of Queen for its distinctive chime and crunch.
Bob Dylan shocked folk purists by going electric at the Newport Folk Festival on July 25, 1965.
Stevie Ray Vaughan played extremely heavy guitar strings, using gauges as thick as .013 on his high E string.
The 12-string guitar creates a natural chorus effect and was prominently used by The Byrds and Led Zeppelin.
The psychedelic era of the late 1960s saw bands experimenting with feedback, tape loops, and Eastern instruments.
Led Zeppelin IV has no official title, band name, or any text on its cover—just four mysterious symbols.
The Hammond organ, originally designed for churches, became a staple of rock music through players like Jon Lord of Deep Purple.
John Lennon was assassinated outside his New York apartment on December 8, 1980.
Live Aid on July 13, 1985, was watched by an estimated 1.9 billion people across 150 countries.
The Fender Telecaster, introduced in 1950, was the first commercially successful solid-body electric guitar.
Queen's Brian May built his iconic guitar, the Red Special, from scratch with his father using wood from an old fireplace mantel.
Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Alabama recorded hits for The Rolling Stones, Lynyrd Skynyrd, and many other rock legends.
Progressive rock bands like Yes, Genesis, and King Crimson created elaborate, symphonic compositions that pushed rock boundaries.
The Gibson Flying V, introduced in 1958, was considered too futuristic but later became an icon of hard rock and metal.
Buddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, and The Big Bopper died in a plane crash on February 3, 1959, known as 'The Day the Music Died.'
AC/DC's Angus Young performs in a schoolboy uniform, a look suggested by his sister Margaret.
Eric Clapton has been inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame three times—as a solo artist and as a member of The Yardbirds and Cream.
The concept album became a defining format of classic rock, with Pink Floyd's 'The Wall' telling a complete narrative across two discs.
Ludwig drums were famously used by Ringo Starr of The Beatles, whose kit appeared on The Ed Sullivan Show in 1964.
Electric Lady Studios in New York was built by Jimi Hendrix and opened shortly before his death in 1970.
The Rolling Stones' Altamont Free Concert on December 6, 1969, turned violent when the Hells Angels, hired as security, killed a fan.
Fleetwood Mac went through numerous lineup changes before Lindsey Buckingham and Stevie Nicks joined in 1975.
Southern rock blended blues, country, and rock, with bands like Lynyrd Skynyrd and The Allman Brothers Band leading the movement.
The tapping technique, popularized by Eddie Van Halen, involves hammering onto the fretboard with both hands to create rapid note sequences.
Roger Dean's fantastical album cover art for Yes became inseparable from the progressive rock aesthetic of the 1970s.
David Bowie's 'Space Oddity' was released just days before the Apollo 11 moon landing in July 1969.
The Moog synthesizer, introduced in the late 1960s, was adopted by bands like Emerson, Lake & Palmer and Pink Floyd.
Atlantic Records was home to Led Zeppelin, Cream, and many other legendary classic rock acts.
Pete Townshend of The Who pioneered the windmill strumming technique and was one of the first to use guitar feedback as a musical tool.
Freddie Mercury's vocal range reportedly spanned four octaves, from bass low F to soprano high F.
Tube amplifiers are preferred by many classic rock guitarists for their warm, natural distortion compared to solid-state amps.
Janis Joplin died of a heroin overdose on October 4, 1970, at age 27.
The Beatles' 'Abbey Road' album cover, showing the four members crossing a zebra crossing, has become one of the most parodied images in music.
Sun Records in Memphis launched the careers of Elvis Presley, Johnny Cash, and Jerry Lee Lewis in the 1950s.
Led Zeppelin's drummer John Bonham was known for using the largest drum kit in rock, including a massive 26-inch bass drum.
Fuzz pedals, first heard on The Rolling Stones' 'Satisfaction,' create a distorted, buzzing tone that defined 1960s rock.
Bruce Springsteen's 'Born in the U.S.A.' is widely misinterpreted as patriotic, but it's actually a protest about the treatment of Vietnam veterans.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in Cleveland, Ohio, opened on September 2, 1995.
Pink Floyd performed the first-ever quadraphonic surround sound concert in 1967.
The Last Waltz, The Band's farewell concert on Thanksgiving 1976, was directed by Martin Scorsese.
Arena rock emerged in the 1970s as bands like Journey, Boston, and Foreigner played to stadium-sized audiences with anthemic songs.
Jimi Hendrix played 'The Star-Spangled Banner' at Woodstock on August 18, 1969, turning it into a psychedelic anti-war statement.
The hammer-on technique allows guitarists to sound a note by tapping the fretboard without picking the string again.
Black Sabbath's Tony Iommi lost the tips of two fingers in a factory accident and created custom thimbles to continue playing guitar.
Bob Dylan was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2016, the first musician to receive the honor.
Elton John has sold more than 300 million records worldwide, making him one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
The concert poster art of the 1960s San Francisco scene, created by artists like Wes Wilson, became a hallmark of psychedelic culture.
The Eagles' 'Their Greatest Hits (1971–1975)' was the first album certified platinum by the RIAA.
Keith Richards of The Rolling Stones uses open G tuning on many songs, removing the low E string from his guitar entirely.
Deep Purple was listed in the Guinness Book of World Records as the world's loudest band in 1972.
Stevie Ray Vaughan died in a helicopter crash on August 27, 1990, after performing alongside Eric Clapton.
DW (Drum Workshop) drums became the go-to brand for rock drummers starting in the 1980s, known for their powerful sound and craftsmanship.
The Beatles released 12 studio albums in just seven years, from 1963 to 1970.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Rumours' has sold over 40 million copies worldwide, making it one of the best-selling albums in history.
The British Invasion's second wave included bands like The Kinks, The Animals, and The Zombies, who brought a harder edge to rock.
Tom Petty refused to let his record label raise the price of his album 'Hard Promises' above $8.98 in 1981, threatening to rename it 'Eight Ninety-Eight.'
Pink Floyd's 'The Wall' concerts featured a massive wall being built across the stage during the performance.
The Rickenbacker 12-string guitar got its iconic jangly sound through The Byrds' Roger McGuinn and George Harrison of The Beatles.
Aerosmith's collaboration with Run-DMC on 'Walk This Way' in 1986 helped bridge rock and hip-hop.
Led Zeppelin's Robert Plant drew heavily from J.R.R. Tolkien's works for lyrical inspiration, referencing Middle-earth in songs like 'Ramble On.'
The Isle of Wight Festival in 1970 drew over 600,000 people, making it one of the largest gatherings of the era.
Neil Young's 'Harvest' was the best-selling album of 1972, featuring the hit 'Heart of Gold.'
Creedence Clearwater Revival's John Fogerty wrote, sang, and played lead guitar on nearly all the band's songs.
The groupie culture of the 1960s and 1970s was a significant part of the rock scene, immortalized by the movie 'Almost Famous.'
Jimi Hendrix died on September 18, 1970, in London at the age of 27.
Queen performed at Live Aid on July 13, 1985, in what is often called the greatest live rock performance of all time.
The distortion effect, created by overdriving a tube amplifier, became the signature sound of rock guitar in the 1960s and beyond.
Gibson introduced the Les Paul Custom in 1954 as a more upscale version of the Standard, featuring gold hardware and an ebony fretboard.
The Allman Brothers Band's 'At Fillmore East' is considered one of the greatest live albums ever recorded.
David Bowie released 'The Rise and Fall of Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars' on June 16, 1972.
Black Sabbath originally formed as a blues band called Earth before changing their name and inventing heavy metal.
The Rolling Stones have been touring for over 60 years, making them the longest-active major rock band in history.
Eric Clapton's 'Layla' was inspired by his unrequited love for George Harrison's wife, Pattie Boyd.
The Who's 'Tommy' (1969) was one of the first rock operas, telling the story of a deaf, dumb, and blind boy.
MTV launched on August 1, 1981, with the Buggles' 'Video Killed the Radio Star,' ushering in a new era for rock music.
Pink Floyd's 'Wish You Were Here' was partly a tribute to founding member Syd Barrett, who left the band due to mental health issues.
Feedback, the high-pitched squeal from an amplifier, was first used intentionally by The Beatles on 'I Feel Fine' in 1964.
Bruce Springsteen's E Street Band has been called the hardest-working band in rock, with concerts often exceeding three hours.
The guitar solo in 'Hotel California' by the Eagles was performed by Don Felder and Joe Walsh playing dueling leads.
Lynyrd Skynyrd's plane crashed on October 20, 1977, killing lead singer Ronnie Van Zant and guitarist Steve Gaines.
The Blues had a profound influence on classic rock, with artists like Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf, and Robert Johnson serving as key inspirations.
Led Zeppelin's 'Kashmir' features no blues influence, instead drawing from Moroccan and Indian musical traditions.
John Bonham of Led Zeppelin died on September 25, 1980, and the band dissolved rather than continue without him.
The Fender Stratocaster's contoured body was revolutionary in 1954, making it more comfortable to play than the flat-bodied Telecaster.
Queen's 'We Will Rock You' stomp-stomp-clap pattern was designed by Brian May as a way for stadium audiences to participate.
Cream, featuring Eric Clapton, Jack Bruce, and Ginger Baker, is considered the first rock supergroup.
The concept of the guitar hero emerged in the 1960s with players like Hendrix, Clapton, and Jeff Beck pushing the instrument's boundaries.
Pink Floyd used a 40-foot inflatable pig for their 'Animals' album cover shoot at Battersea Power Station. The pig broke free and disrupted air traffic.
Tom Petty's 'Full Moon Fever' was initially rejected by MCA Records before becoming a multi-platinum success.
The Doors' Jim Morrison was arrested on stage in New Haven, Connecticut, becoming the first rock musician arrested during a performance.
AC/DC's original vocalist Bon Scott died on February 19, 1980, and was replaced by Brian Johnson.
Rock fashion in the 1970s included bell-bottoms, platform shoes, leather jackets, and band t-shirts that remain iconic today.
Deep Purple's 'Machine Head' was recorded in a mobile studio parked outside a casino in Montreux, Switzerland.
Elton John performed at the Troubadour in Los Angeles on August 25, 1970, launching his career in America.
The Beatles' 'Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band' is often cited as the first concept album in rock history.
Aerosmith's Steven Tyler and Joe Perry were nicknamed the 'Toxic Twins' due to their heavy drug use during the 1970s.
Stevie Ray Vaughan's debut album 'Texas Flood' was recorded in just three days.
The Gibson ES-335, a semi-hollow body guitar, was favored by blues-rock players like B.B. King, Chuck Berry, and Eric Clapton.
Fleetwood Mac's 'The Chain' features a bass line by John McVie that has become one of the most recognizable in rock.
Bob Dylan's 'Like a Rolling Stone' revolutionized rock music with its six-minute length and poetic lyrics.
The Who's Pete Townshend developed severe tinnitus and hearing loss from decades of performing at high volumes.
Led Zeppelin's manager Peter Grant was notorious for his intimidating tactics to protect the band's interests.
Janis Joplin's 'Me and Bobby McGee,' released posthumously, became her only number-one single.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inducted its first class in 1986, including Chuck Berry, Elvis Presley, and Buddy Holly.
Pink Floyd's 'Money' uses a 7/4 time signature in its verses, unusual for a rock song that became a hit.
Bruce Springsteen's 'Born to Run' was released on August 25, 1975, and is considered one of the greatest rock albums ever made.
Keith Moon of The Who once drove a Lincoln Continental into a Holiday Inn swimming pool to celebrate his 21st birthday.
The Marshall 'Plexi' amplifier head, used by Hendrix and many others, got its name from its plexiglass front panel.
Creedence Clearwater Revival only lasted four years as a band (1968-1972) despite releasing seven studio albums in that time.
The Beatles were rejected by Decca Records in 1962, with the label saying guitar groups were 'on the way out.'
Robert Johnson, who allegedly sold his soul to the devil at a Mississippi crossroads, is considered the grandfather of rock guitar.
Neil Young's 'Old Man' was written about the caretaker of his newly purchased ranch in Northern California.
The Rolling Stones' 'Exile on Main St.' was recorded in a basement studio in the south of France while the band was in tax exile.
Queen's 'A Night at the Opera' was the most expensive album ever produced at the time of its release in 1975.
David Bowie had permanently dilated left pupil from a childhood fight, giving the illusion of having two different-colored eyes.
The Eagles broke up in 1980 after a backstage argument so heated that Glenn Frey and Don Felder threatened each other on stage.
Black Sabbath's debut album was recorded in a single day, October 16, 1969.
The wah pedal works by sweeping a band-pass filter across the frequencies, mimicking the human voice.
Led Zeppelin's 'Whole Lotta Love' features one of the most iconic guitar riffs ever recorded.
Elton John has composed music for Broadway and Disney, including 'The Lion King,' which won him an Academy Award.
The Grateful Dead pioneered the idea of allowing fans to record their concerts, creating a massive bootleg trading community.
Pink Floyd's 'Dark Side of the Moon' was followed up with the equally ambitious 'Wish You Were Here' in 1975.
Stevie Ray Vaughan was discovered by David Bowie, who invited him to play on the 'Let's Dance' album.
The British blues boom of the early 1960s saw bands like The Yardbirds, John Mayall's Bluesbreakers, and Fleetwood Mac drawing from American blues.
AC/DC took their name from a sewing machine label that read 'AC/DC,' standing for alternating current/direct current.
Aerosmith's 'Dream On' took years to become a hit, initially charting modestly in 1973 before becoming a classic.
The Doors recorded their debut album in just six days at Sunset Sound Recorders in Hollywood.
Queen recorded 'We Are the Champions' and 'We Will Rock You' as a paired single. Together they became the ultimate stadium anthems.
Tom Petty fought his record label to keep album prices affordable and won, earning respect throughout the music industry.
Freddie Mercury died on November 24, 1991, one day after publicly announcing he had AIDS.
The Marshall stack, consisting of a head and multiple speaker cabinets, became the visual symbol of rock power.
Fleetwood Mac's Peter Green, the original founder, left the band in 1970 after a bad LSD experience permanently altered his personality.
Classic rock radio as a format was pioneered by stations like KMET in Los Angeles and WNEW-FM in New York in the late 1960s.
Pink Floyd performed 'The Dark Side of the Moon' live in its entirety before any of it was recorded in the studio.
The Rolling Stones' 'Sticky Fingers' album cover, designed by Andy Warhol, featured a working zipper on the original vinyl release.
Led Zeppelin avoided releasing singles in the UK, insisting fans buy the full album to hear their music.
Eric Clapton's 'Crossroads' at Cream's farewell concert is often cited as one of the greatest live guitar performances ever.
The Grammy Award for Best Rock Album was first awarded in 1995 to Tom Petty's 'Wildflowers.'
Lynyrd Skynyrd's 'Sweet Home Alabama' was written as a response to Neil Young's 'Southern Man' and 'Alabama.'
Jimi Hendrix Experience released three studio albums in just two years before disbanding in 1969.
The Beatles' 'White Album' was so diverse in style that it contained everything from hard rock to avant-garde sound collages.
Deep Purple's vocalist Ian Gillan also played Jesus in the original recording of 'Jesus Christ Superstar.'
Bob Dylan released 'Highway 61 Revisited' on August 30, 1965, featuring the iconic 'Like a Rolling Stone.'
Black Sabbath's Ozzy Osbourne was fired from the band in 1979 and went on to a hugely successful solo career.
The Gibson Les Paul weighs around 9 pounds, significantly heavier than the Fender Stratocaster, contributing to its thick, sustaining tone.
Woodstock's three days of peace and music took place from August 15-18, 1969, despite only being planned for two days.
Neil Young was a founding member of Buffalo Springfield before going solo and joining Crosby, Stills, Nash & Young.
The Rolling Stones' tongue and lips logo, designed by John Pasche in 1970, is one of the most recognizable logos in rock history.
Queen's 'Bohemian Rhapsody' took three weeks to record, which was unusually long for a single song in 1975.
Pete Townshend of The Who wrote the rock opera 'Quadrophenia' about the mod subculture of 1960s London.
David Bowie died on January 10, 2016, just two days after releasing his final album 'Blackstar.'
The 'Wall of Sound' production technique, developed by Phil Spector, influenced rock producers throughout the classic era.
Led Zeppelin's 'Houses of the Holy' album cover, photographed at the Giant's Causeway in Northern Ireland, caused controversy for depicting nude children.
Fleetwood Mac's Stevie Nicks wrote 'Landslide' while contemplating whether to stay in music or return to school.
The Blues Brothers movie (1980) helped introduce a new generation to classic blues and rock artists like Ray Charles and Aretha Franklin.
AC/DC's 'Back in Black' was a tribute to their late singer Bon Scott, with the album's black cover representing mourning.
Pink Floyd's Roger Waters performed 'The Wall' at the site of the fallen Berlin Wall in 1990, with an audience of 350,000.
Bruce Springsteen wrote 'Born to Run' in six months, obsessively reworking it until it captured the sound in his head.
The Band's Robbie Robertson decided to stop touring in 1976, leading to The Last Waltz and the group's eventual breakup.
Classic rock concert t-shirts have become collectible items, with vintage shirts from bands like Led Zeppelin selling for thousands of dollars.
Eric Clapton played a psychedelic-painted Gibson SG called 'The Fool' during his time with Cream.
The Eagles' 'Hotel California' guitar riff was partially inspired by Jethro Tull's 'We Used to Know.'
Aerosmith broke up in the early 1980s due to substance abuse but reunited and had an even bigger comeback in the late 1980s and 1990s.
Pink Floyd's 'Animals' divided its songs into three categories—Dogs, Pigs, and Sheep—as a commentary on society.
The Jimi Hendrix Experience's 'Are You Experienced' was released on May 12, 1967, in the UK.
The Rolling Stones recorded at Muscle Shoals Sound Studios in Alabama, where they cut 'Brown Sugar' and 'Wild Horses.'
Tom Petty died on October 2, 2017, at the age of 66, from an accidental prescription drug overdose.
Creedence Clearwater Revival's 'Fortunate Son' became an anthem of the anti-Vietnam War movement.
The Beatles' producer George Martin was known as the 'Fifth Beatle' for his crucial role in shaping their sound.
Led Zeppelin's 'Physical Graffiti' was a double album that showcased the band's range from hard rock to acoustic folk.
Deep Purple's 'Highway Star' features one of the most technically impressive organ solos in rock history, played by Jon Lord.
Queen guitarist Brian May earned a PhD in astrophysics from Imperial College London in 2007.
The Fender Jazzmaster, while designed for jazz, was adopted by surf and alternative rock musicians.
Bob Dylan's 'Blowin' in the Wind' became an anthem of the civil rights movement in the 1960s.
The Rolling Stones' 1969 U.S. tour was the first major rock tour to use advanced lighting and sound systems.
Elton John wore some of the most extravagant costumes in rock history, including sequined Dodgers uniforms and feathered outfits.
Black Sabbath's 'War Pigs' was originally titled 'Walpurgis' and contained even darker lyrics about witchcraft.
Pink Floyd's 'Comfortably Numb' guitar solo by David Gilmour is consistently voted one of the greatest solos ever.
The Who released 'Who's Next' on August 14, 1971, featuring the revolutionary use of synthesizers on tracks like 'Baba O'Riley.'
Janis Joplin left $2,500 in her will for a posthumous party at her favorite bar, stating 'drinks are on Pearl.'
Stevie Ray Vaughan's guitar 'Number One,' a battered 1963 Stratocaster, became one of the most famous guitars in blues-rock.
The Moody Blues' 'Days of Future Passed' (1967) was one of the first albums to combine rock with a full orchestra.
Led Zeppelin's 'Immigrant Song' was inspired by the band's 1970 concert in Iceland.
The Beatles released 'Let It Be' on May 8, 1970, a month after the band officially broke up.
AC/DC's 'Thunderstruck' opens with one of the most instantly recognizable guitar intros in rock, played entirely on one string.
Jeff Beck, Jimmy Page, and Eric Clapton all played guitar for The Yardbirds at different times, making it a legendary training ground.
The Eagles' 'Desperado' album was a concept record themed around the Old West outlaw life.
Classic rock vinyl collecting has seen a massive resurgence, with first pressings of iconic albums fetching tens of thousands of dollars.
Jethro Tull's Ian Anderson played flute standing on one leg, becoming one of rock's most distinctive performers.
The Beatles' 'Revolution 9' is an eight-minute avant-garde sound collage that bewildered fans upon its release.
Pink Floyd's 'Dark Side of the Moon' used innovative recording techniques, including multi-track tape loops and sound effects.
Bruce Springsteen's 'Thunder Road' opens with a harmonica intro and has become one of the most beloved opening tracks in rock.
Fleetwood Mac's Christine McVie wrote many of the band's biggest hits, including 'Don't Stop' and 'Everywhere.'
The Who's 'My Generation' features Roger Daltrey's stuttering vocal delivery, which was meant to represent the frustrations of youth.
Led Zeppelin's Jimmy Page used a violin bow on his guitar during live performances of 'Dazed and Confused.'
Elton John performed at Dodger Stadium in Los Angeles in October 1975, wearing a sequined Dodgers uniform in one of rock's most iconic concerts.
Neil Young's 'Rust Never Sleeps' combined acoustic and electric sets, influencing the grunge movement that followed.
Bob Dylan won the Grammy for Album of the Year in 1998 for 'Time Out of Mind,' marking a major comeback.
The Who were the first band to use laser lights in a live concert production.
Pink Floyd's 'Echoes' from the 'Meddle' album runs 23 minutes and showcases the band's experimental side.
Led Zeppelin IV is estimated to have sold over 37 million copies worldwide.
Black Sabbath released their debut album in the UK on Friday, February 13, 1970, deliberately choosing the ominous date.
Aerosmith's 'Toys in the Attic' (1975) elevated them from regional act to international superstars.
The Doors were banned from The Ed Sullivan Show after Jim Morrison defied producers and sang the unedited lyrics to 'Light My Fire.'
Eric Clapton's 'Unplugged' album (1992) became the best-selling live album in the U.S. and revived the acoustic format.
The Rolling Stones' 'Let It Bleed' was released on December 5, 1969, the day before the Altamont disaster.
Queen's four members each had at least one song on the flip side of a number-one single, a unique distinction.
David Bowie acted in over 25 films, including 'The Man Who Fell to Earth' and 'Labyrinth.'
The Gibson Firebird, designed by car designer Ray Dietrich, brought automotive aesthetics to guitar design in 1963.
Lynyrd Skynyrd were named after Leonard Skinner, a strict gym teacher who punished students for having long hair.
Pink Floyd's 'The Division Bell' tour in 1994 set records for the highest-grossing rock tour at the time.
Tom Petty joined the Traveling Wilburys supergroup alongside Bob Dylan, George Harrison, Roy Orbison, and Jeff Lynne.
Led Zeppelin's 'In Through the Out Door' was their last studio album, released just over a year before John Bonham's death.
The Rolling Stones have released 30 studio albums over their career, more than almost any other rock band.
Deep Purple's Ritchie Blackmore was one of the first rock guitarists to incorporate classical music influences into his playing.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Go Your Own Way' was written by Lindsey Buckingham about his breakup with Stevie Nicks while they were still in the same band.
The Beatles recorded their debut album 'Please Please Me' in a single marathon session on February 11, 1963.
AC/DC's 'Highway to Hell' was released just months before Bon Scott's death, making its title eerily prophetic.
Queen's 'Under Pressure,' a collaboration with David Bowie, was written and recorded in a single session.
Bruce Springsteen played his first concert at age 15 at a drive-in restaurant in Freehold, New Jersey.
The concept of headlining a festival was largely established during the classic rock era, with bands competing for top billing.
Stevie Ray Vaughan was a major factor in the blues revival of the 1980s, bringing Texas blues to mainstream audiences.
The Allman Brothers Band's Duane Allman died in a motorcycle accident on October 29, 1971, at age 24.
Jimi Hendrix left behind hundreds of hours of unreleased recordings, which have been compiled into numerous posthumous albums.
The Beatles' 'Yesterday' holds the record for the most cover versions of any song ever written, with over 2,200 recorded versions.
Pink Floyd's 'Pulse' concert tour in 1994 featured a massive circular screen and an elaborate light show.
The Rolling Stones were arrested in the 1960s and 1970s on drug charges, fueling their rebellious image.
Led Zeppelin's 'Black Dog' gets its name from a black Labrador retriever that wandered around Headley Grange studio.
Neil Young recorded 'Harvest Moon' as a companion piece to 'Harvest,' 20 years after the original album.
Bob Dylan caused controversy by going electric at Newport in 1965, with folk purists booing his performance.
The Eagles' Greatest Hits compilation has sold over 38 million copies in the U.S. alone.
Pink Floyd's Roger Waters went on to perform 'The Wall' in stadiums worldwide as a solo touring production.
Cream only existed for two years (1966-1968) but recorded four albums and influenced generations of rock musicians.
Black Sabbath's 'Paranoid' was written in minutes as a last-minute addition to fill the album and became their biggest hit.
The Gibson Hummingbird acoustic guitar has been used by everyone from Keith Richards to Sheryl Crow.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame requires artists to wait 25 years after their first recording before becoming eligible for induction.
Queen's 'Somebody to Love' featured the band members layering their voices to create the effect of a 100-person gospel choir.
David Bowie's 'Heroes' was inspired by seeing his producer Tony Visconti kissing a woman by the Berlin Wall.
AC/DC's Malcolm Young, often overshadowed by his brother Angus, was considered one of rock's greatest rhythm guitarists.
The Monterey Pop Festival in June 1967 is considered the beginning of the 'Summer of Love.'
Tom Petty's 'American Girl' was rumored to be about a girl who jumped off a university building, but Petty denied it.
The Who destroyed over $50,000 worth of equipment during their career through their famously destructive performances.
Led Zeppelin's 'When the Levee Breaks' drum sound was recorded at the bottom of a stairwell in Headley Grange, creating its massive reverb.
Pink Floyd was one of the first bands to use a custom PA system, designing their own sound equipment for concerts.
Fleetwood Mac was founded in 1967 by Peter Green, who named the band after the rhythm section: Mick Fleetwood and John McVie.
Eric Clapton's 'Tears in Heaven' was written about the death of his four-year-old son, who fell from a 53rd-floor window.
The Beatles' manager Brian Epstein cleaned up their image, putting them in suits, but John Lennon always resisted.
Deep Purple's 'Child in Time' features Ian Gillan's vocal range pushed to its limits over a ten-minute epic.
Janis Joplin bought a psychedelic-painted Porsche 356C that has become one of the most valuable rock memorabilia items.
The Band's 'The Weight' was inspired by the films of Luis Buñuel and the American South.
Elton John and Bernie Taupin have one of the longest-running songwriting partnerships in pop and rock history, starting in 1967.
Black Sabbath's name was inspired by a Boris Karloff horror film, reflecting their interest in dark themes.
Pink Floyd's 'Dark Side of the Moon' was the first album to be mixed using a quadraphonic sound system.
Aerosmith's Joe Perry was ranked as one of the top 100 guitarists of all time by Rolling Stone magazine.
The Beatles' final recording session took place on January 4, 1970, at Abbey Road Studios.
Neil Young refused to let his music be used in commercials for decades, believing it would compromise artistic integrity.
Creedence Clearwater Revival's 'Proud Mary' was covered by Ike and Tina Turner in a gritty version that became even more famous.
Bruce Springsteen wrote 'Dancing in the Dark' at the request of his manager Jon Landau, who felt the album needed a hit single.
The Rolling Stones' Mick Jagger studied at the London School of Economics before pursuing music full-time.
Led Zeppelin never allowed their songs to appear in TV commercials during the band's active years.
Jeff Beck refused to play the same setlist twice, preferring to improvise and constantly reinvent his performances.
Pink Floyd's Syd Barrett showed up at the studio during the recording of 'Wish You Were Here,' so changed in appearance that the band didn't recognize him at first.
The Eagles' Joe Walsh was known for his wild hotel room antics, once chainsaw-ing a hotel room before his stay so he could complain it was trashed upon arrival.
Stevie Ray Vaughan's guitar tone was achieved partly through his use of heavy strings and an incredibly forceful playing style.
The Jimi Hendrix Experience's 'Electric Ladyland' was a double album that reached number one on the U.S. charts.
Queen's 'Killer Queen' was the first song that brought the band mainstream success, reaching number two in the UK in 1974.
David Bowie released 27 studio albums over a career spanning five decades, constantly reinventing his musical style.
George Harrison released 'All Things Must Pass' after The Beatles broke up, a triple album that outsold any solo Beatles release.
Led Zeppelin's concert at Knebworth in 1979 drew over 200,000 fans, one of the largest outdoor concerts in UK history.
The Marshall JCM800 amplifier, introduced in 1981, became the go-to amp for hard rock and metal guitarists.
Pink Floyd released 'Dark Side of the Moon' on March 1, 1973, and it went on to become one of the best-selling albums of all time.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Tusk' was recorded partly with the USC Trojan Marching Band, costing over $1 million to produce.
The Doors never had a bass player—Ray Manzarek played bass lines on a keyboard with his left hand.
Bob Dylan's 'Blonde on Blonde' (1966) was rock's first double album.
Black Sabbath reunited with Ozzy Osbourne for the album '13' in 2013, topping charts worldwide.
The Rolling Stones' Charlie Watts was a jazz drummer at heart who brought a unique swing to rock and roll.
Aerosmith's 'I Don't Want to Miss a Thing' from the 'Armageddon' soundtrack became their first and only number-one single.
Eric Clapton was known as 'God' in 1960s London, with fans writing 'Clapton is God' as graffiti across the city.
Queen's 'Don't Stop Me Now' has been scientifically determined to be the most feel-good song ever recorded.
Led Zeppelin IV sold over 37 million copies despite having no title, no band name, and no text printed on its cover.
The Beatles used Indian instruments like the sitar on 'Norwegian Wood,' popularizing world music influences in rock.
Neil Young's 'Hey Hey, My My' contains the famous line 'It's better to burn out than to fade away,' later quoted in Kurt Cobain's suicide note.
The Fender Rhodes electric piano, while not a guitar, became an essential keyboard in 1970s rock recordings.
Pink Floyd's 'Another Brick in the Wall Part 2' used a children's choir from Islington Green School, who were never properly compensated.
Tom Petty's 'Learning to Fly' was about learning to pilot an airplane, inspired by Petty's flying lessons.
The Rolling Stones' 'Gimme Shelter' features backing vocalist Merry Clayton, whose powerful vocals caused her to miscarry shortly after the session.
AC/DC's debut album in Australia, 'High Voltage,' featured a completely different track listing than the international release.
Queen's 'Crazy Little Thing Called Love' was written by Freddie Mercury in the bath in about ten minutes.
Hendrix, though primarily known for his Stratocaster work, also played Flying V guitars in his later career.
The Beatles' 'Helter Skelter' is often cited as one of the first heavy metal songs, with Paul McCartney aiming to make the loudest, dirtiest song possible.
The Kinks were banned from touring the United States from 1965 to 1969 due to disputes with the American musicians' union.
Pink Floyd's 'Atom Heart Mother' featured a full orchestra and choir, pushing the boundaries of what rock could be.
Led Zeppelin's Jimmy Page was one of the most sought-after session guitarists in London before forming the band.
Elton John has performed over 4,000 concerts in more than 80 countries during his career.
The Who's 'Baba O'Riley' was named after spiritual leader Meher Baba and composer Terry Riley.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Rumours' spent 31 weeks at number one on the Billboard 200 in 1977-1978.
Bruce Springsteen's 'The River' was a double album exploring working-class life in New Jersey.
The Gibson Explorer, like the Flying V, was initially a commercial failure in 1958 but later became a favorite of hard rock guitarists.
Deep Purple once held the Guinness World Record for the loudest rock concert, measured at 117 dB in London in 1972.
Led Zeppelin refused to appear on Top of the Pops, the BBC's flagship music show, throughout their career.
Pink Floyd released 'The Wall' on November 30, 1979, telling the story of a rock star's mental breakdown.
Janis Joplin's vocal style was heavily influenced by Bessie Smith and other classic blues singers.
AC/DC was formed in Sydney, Australia, in 1973 by Scottish-born brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.
The Beatles' 'A Day in the Life' features a 40-piece orchestra building to a crescendo, organized by producer George Martin.
Bob Dylan's 'The Times They Are a-Changin'' became the anthem of the 1960s counterculture movement.
The Allman Brothers Band pioneered the twin-guitar harmony technique that became a hallmark of Southern rock.
Aerosmith was discovered by Clive Davis of Columbia Records, who signed them after seeing a gig in a Boston club.
Queen's 'Bohemian Rhapsody' returned to number one in 2018 after the biopic film about the band was released.
Keith Richards once fell asleep during a recording session and accidentally created the riff for 'Satisfaction' on a tape recorder.
The Gibson ES-175 jazz guitar influenced early rock and roll players who crossed genre boundaries.
Led Zeppelin's 'The Song Remains the Same' was both a concert film and a live album recorded at Madison Square Garden.
Pink Floyd's 'Shine On You Crazy Diamond' is a 26-minute tribute to original frontman Syd Barrett.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Dreams' by Stevie Nicks went viral on TikTok in 2020, introducing the band to a new generation.
Led Zeppelin was sued multiple times for allegedly borrowing from blues songs without credit, including for 'Stairway to Heaven.'
The Rolling Stones released 'Beggars Banquet' in 1968, marking a return to their blues roots after the psychedelic era.
Bruce Springsteen performed a legendary four-hour concert at the Meadowlands in New Jersey in 1985.
Queen's Brian May uses a sixpence coin instead of a guitar pick, giving him a distinctive attack and tone.
The Beatles performed on The Ed Sullivan Show on February 9, 1964, watched by 73 million viewers.
Deep Purple's 'Burn' showcased the band's fusion of classical and hard rock in the mid-1970s.
The Moog synthesizer was used by Keith Emerson of Emerson, Lake & Palmer to bring classical compositions into the rock arena.
Tom Petty burned down his childhood home during an argument with his father when he was a teenager.
Stevie Ray Vaughan often played through two amplifiers simultaneously to achieve his massive guitar tone.
The Who sold over 100 million records worldwide and are considered one of the most influential bands of the 20th century.
Pink Floyd's 'Learning to Fly' was inspired by David Gilmour's passion for aviation and flying.
Elton John's 'Goodbye Yellow Brick Road' album contained 17 tracks across two vinyl records and spawned four hit singles.
The Beatles' 'Let It Be' title track was inspired by Paul McCartney's dream about his late mother, Mary.
Black Sabbath's 'Iron Man' riff is one of the most recognizable in all of heavy metal and hard rock.
The Marshall JMP-1 preamp allowed guitarists to get Marshall tone at lower volumes in studio recordings.
Led Zeppelin's 'Going to California' was inspired by Joni Mitchell, whom Robert Plant and Jimmy Page greatly admired.
Pink Floyd was named after two blues musicians: Pink Anderson and Floyd Council.
Led Zeppelin's 'Rock and Roll' was written in just 15 minutes during a jam session at Headley Grange.
The Rolling Stones' 'Paint It Black' features a sitar played by Brian Jones, blending Indian music with rock.
Queen's 'A Kind of Magic' was written for the soundtrack of the film 'Highlander' in 1986.
Eric Clapton auctioned off over 100 of his guitars in 2004, raising nearly $6 million for his Crossroads Centre addiction treatment facility.
Deep Purple released 'Made in Japan' in 1972, which is widely regarded as one of the greatest live rock albums ever recorded.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Tango in the Night' (1987) was the last album to feature Lindsey Buckingham until he returned in 1997.
Bruce Springsteen's 'Nebraska' was recorded alone on a four-track cassette recorder in his bedroom, giving it a stark, haunting quality.
The Beatles' 'Come Together' was originally written by John Lennon as a campaign song for Timothy Leary's California gubernatorial race.
Aerosmith holds the record for the most gold and multi-platinum albums by an American group.
Elton John performed over 3,500 concerts between 1970 and 2023, including his record-breaking Farewell Yellow Brick Road tour.
The Kinks' 'You Really Got Me' (1964) is often credited as one of the first hard rock songs due to Dave Davies slashing his amp speaker with a razor blade.
Santana's performance at Woodstock launched Carlos Santana to stardom, even though their debut album hadn't yet been released.
Led Zeppelin's 'No Quarter' features John Paul Jones on keyboards, creating an eerie, atmospheric sound that was years ahead of its time.
AC/DC's 'For Those About to Rock (We Salute You)' used real cannons during live performances for dramatic effect.
